headlines.title: Garner's Plan
headlines.fullText:
The man and the occasion have met again in the face of a great emergency. It has been plain for some weeks that the lobbyists at Washington must be drastically treated, but just how to shape the regulation has been a puzzle to many statesmen-to many but not to all. To the mind of Representative Garner the proper proceeding shines out as bright as a new tin bucket, and has no doubt so shome from the first. Mr. Garner would compel all lobbyists to get a license and wear a license number on their persons. In the part of Texas where Mr. Garner has made his home are thousands of sheep and many, many goats, and he has seen 'em in little. big and medium flocks, especially the goats, cropping the tender grass blades and flaunting their faunal whiskers in the morning sun. In fact, It is said one of the Texas poets of passion has produced a bunch-grass lyric beginning: "In the spring John Garner's fancy lightly turns to thoughts of goats." But let that go, The point is that afr. Garner has looked much upon the goat and the sheep, and has not looked in vain. Vartous herdmasters have a way of marking sheep and goats for identification of ownership by fastening a metal tag to their cars with a link of baling wire. This Texas custom no doubt flashed on his mind at once when the problem of marking or branding lobbyists came Up. True, he has not proposed to apply the stockmen's method In Its crude state. Perhaps he regards it as too coarse and cruel, and suggests badges of buttons in place of pewter tass. tags. But in this is he not overscrupulous, kind and considerate? Certainly a wicked lobbyist has no more claim to humane treatment than the harmless, necessary goat. It would not hurt any hardened lobbyist much to pierce one of his auriculars and brad in his license tag. In fact, both ears could be bored and two tags attached and worn as ear-bobbs, one of them showing the proper facts of his permit and the other designating his accustomed range-house or senate. But whatever modified form of marking and branding may finally be adopted, the idea in a republic of free men 's certainly great and will not be allowed to get away. It is contribution of the wide and rolling range sections to the unimaginative East that cannot fail to implant itself in the publie mind and In the statute books. In the near and more fumigated future the street urchins of Washington may be chanting a variation from Mother Goose something like this:
"Hark! Hark! The dogs do bark:
The Lobs are coming to town
And all wear tags:
they can't drag our statesmen down-
Can't drag - our rock-ribbed, high-souled patriots down."

headlines.title: Free Ice for the Needy
headlines.subtitle: By Frederic J. Haskin.
headlines.fullText:
When Saladin sent snow from the tops of Lebanon to the wounded Richard of the Lion Heart, he built for himself Imperishable tame as a generous for, for in that time and for centuries thereafter Ice in summer was a luxury that only kings and great captains might hope for. Nowadays, In America, at any rate ice is Bu longer a luxury for city dwellers. but a necessary thing, something that everybody above the direst poverty buys; something charity justly fecla must be furnished the very poof.
The recent strike that closed the ice factories of Cincinnati caused such terrible suffering among the babies and poorer people that the mayor felt Justified In selzing the ice plants and running them in the name of the city, pending the settlement of labor trouble. The Ice companies protested with an application for an injunction, but when the mayor showed that he had attempted to obtain ice from other cities and fulled, the court refused for the time being to interfere. The government of this one American city took the position that ice was an absolute necessity, and tout the municipal government could not permit the supply to be stopped, even if forced to resort to the most extreme measures.
Of course, Americans use ice much more freely than do any other people, so freely that Europeans affect eupprise that we are able at all to survive the copious draughts of ice water we dally consume, but ice as a preservative of food and for use in the sick room Is looked upon as necessary in every country in the now world.
The housing conditions in American cities "perhaps make its use more imperative here than in other countries. That it is looked upon as almost on a par with water is proved by the fact that President McAneny of the Borough of Manhattan not long ago seriously proposed the municipalisation of all plants of New York City.
A few weeks earlier it developed that the "ice trust" in New York City without ostentation, and without any purpose that the fact ever should become public, had been engaged for years in giving away hundreda of thousands of tons of ice every summer, the companies basing their action on the conviction that everybody in New York should have a bit of ice every day whether able to pay for it or not.
How much the world owes for baby lives saved, for fevers cooled, for food preserved, for comfort, to De. Stephen oGree of Apalachicola cannot be estimated. Dr. Goree was the Florida physician who in 1844, in an effort to cool the temperature of the room In which his wife lay ill of fever, fell upon the secret of making artificial ice. It was nearly twenty years later that his invention was perfected and operated in a commercial way, but since then it has carried "The snows from the tops of Lebanon" to sufferers In every clime.
In most of the large cities of the United States the need of ice for the poor has become so generally accepted that special funds are provided in the municipal budget euch year for this purpose. In othera lee is supplied liberally by other organizations, such as the Visiting Nurses, the Associated Charities, and the various sick diet stations, Whever milk is given to a sick baby ice is also likely to be provided to prevent the milk from souring. For this reason Ice and milk are frequently distributed from the same stations. A number of cities have organized Sterilized Milk and Ice Associations with distributing stations in different parts of the city in order that these two relief objects may be as near as possible to the demand.
The methods of distribution of tee vary in different cities and in different years according to the sources of supply and the needs" In some cases ice tickets are distributed which are accepted as payment for a five or ten cent piece of ice if presented at any station where the ice is being distributed. In others an order to supply ice to a certain address is given to the tremes who deliver it with their regular orders. In cases of sudden illness it is obtained through the wid of the visiting nurses or physician. Thousands of tons of ice are each year given to the poor in every large city.
In this connection it should be noted that few dealers in any commodity are as generous in giving their products to the needy as the icemon. Every social worker knows than an appeal for ice to any dealer is likely to meet with a response. Frequently the lee will be given in bulk to meet some sudden emergency. When a boller explosion in southern Philadelphia injured several score of men one summer. only a few of whom were admitted to hospitals, porary station to which 1,000 pounds of ice were dally delivered dally for the benefit of the men and boys suffering from injuries sustained in the explosion.
Sometimes an ice dealer will say to the charity organizations. "I will contribute 1,000 pounds of ice a week If you can arrange for its distribution without calling for extra service." This may call for some planning but usually the organization can arrange to co-operate with some dealer handling the ice regularly who will receive the quantity and allow it to be distributed to those having a tickets or order furnished by the organization. In most towns ice tickets are now to be had through some charitable organization. These will be honored by a dealer as readily as the milk tickets in common use.
A wealthy girl went into the crowded section of New York one morning in quest of someone. She lost her way, had her pocketbook snatched, and then tripped and sprained her ankle. Two poor women working in a hot, stuffy room took pity on her and helped her into their „close quarters, After some difficulty one of them obtained a messenger to take a message to her home. For several hours the girl crouched upon the floor, her ankle throbbing with pain, and her senses almost henumbed by the stifling heat. She begged for a drink of cold water but the lukewarm product from the hydrant repelled her. Yet those poor women working there day never had had anything else. Her father came. to her ald and a few days care restored her ankle, One of her first 'acts afterward was to arrange for & cooler in that house with a daily supply of ice In order that every poor, hard working tenant might at least have within the reach comfort of the Scriptural cup of cold water.
The visiting nurse is most enthusiastle in her approval of the advantage of being able to supply lee when needed to the atck poor. "No one can understand what it means to a nurse," said one of them recently, "to go into a hot, stuffy room where someone is suffering with a fever, and be absolutely without any means of lowering this temperature. Many s time ! have come out ind bought Ice out of my own money because the send was so great I could not bear it. Sometimes a unor girt witt come home to the family her scant sarning" are helping to support. suffering from a blinding headache which seems impossible to bear. The application at an tee Pap, after a ceptd bath, may give her @ good steht's deep and send her back to work the next morning feeling refreshed. Without It she might toas in pain all night and be unfit for work next day. It Is not always possible to have sufficient Ice evon for every fever patient in a hospital, and in many a home ire cannot be supplied without outside ald."
The most grevious tack in a hospital in an insufficient supply of ice, and yet this happens sometime because the noswar deneguired. of its disposal to supply all that is required. A well-to-do man one hot day suffered from severe pain which only the ice bag would relieve. The hospital was out of ice for the day and it was only by communication with outside friends that his need was supplied. He afterward gave a sum of money to that hospital to be devoted exclusively to the supply of ice In order that patients who had no outside resources might never be compelled to suffer from the lack of It.
Those who oppose the distribution of ice to the poor upon the ground that it is giving them a taste for luxuries they should not acquire, unless themselves able to gratify it, are not closely in touch with the manner in which ice is distributed. Few if any receive it just to add to their ordinary comfort or to preserve their food, as do the people in more fortunate circumstances. The destitute wife does not buy food in any greater quantities than the dally need of her family requires. She is seldom able to do even that. But if her baby's milk, which she often is wot able to buy, is to be kept sweet, she may need a dally plece of Ice as well.
The tenement mothers who crowd up to a free ice station upon a hot day are a pathetic group. Usually each is equipped with a goodly supply of newspaper in which the welcome lump of coldness is wrapped carefully in order that it may be kept as long as possible. Most of them have among their scant furniture a wooden box and perhaps an old sack. The lee is placed in the box, the bottle of milk tight against It and the sack covering all until baby's feeding time.
Sometimes a mother will manage to keep back a milk bottle or two which she will fill with water and pack against the ice also in order that the older children may have a cooler drink than would otherwise be possible. Anyone who has seen eager children crowding around their mother as she removes the mack from the cherished lump of Ice, in order that they may have the simple treat of a cup of cold water, cannot but feel that the money spent in giving ice to the poor in hot weather is as good an investment in human comfort and happiness as can be secured.
Tomorrow: CONFERENCE FOR THE COMMON GOOD,

headlines.title: Ponderous Personages
headlines.subtitle: ALEXANDER THE GREAT. By George Fitch, Author of "At Good Old Siwash."
headlines.fullText:
Alexander the Great was born in Macedonia, B. C. 356. He was one of King Philip's sons, No. 13. Series B, and was so handsome and accomplished that his loving father got jealous of him and tried to ventilate him with a spear during a social session of the Amalgamated Sons of Thunder, at which a large number of kegs had been tapped.
At this time Macedonia is being pawed over by many armies to Its great damage, but at that time It was light-weight champion of the fighting world and everybody treated it with respect. When Alexander became regent at the age of 16, he organized the justly celebrated Macedonian phalanx and roamed over his enemies go successfully that in a few months he was boss of Greece and was able to appoint a personal friend postmaster of Athens.
Alexander at this time was beautiful, accomplished and witty. He was a fine swordsman and had been educated by Aristotle. Every Greek letter society in Hellas was crazy to pledge him, but he stuck to business and organized an army for the conquest of Persia,
At this time Persia stretched almost all over the tar east and was fifty times as large an Greece with an ariny which would have made akiser WUheim's look small and lonesome. But Alexander didn't care. He took over 70,000 foot soldiers and 7,000 cavalry and when he met Durtus with a million troops and a whole fleet ot elephants he tunneled through the opposition time and again with entire success Alexan-der's men killed Persians until they got cramps in their wrists and Alexander sent Darius home on ice and wok over the country.
At this time Alexander was 25 years old, and his army showed little sign of wear. He conquered Egypt, Mesopotamia and other miscellaneous countries, but finally ran out of enemies, owing to the extremely limited information contained in the geographies of that time and after weeping bitterly because he had no foes left to swat. he came back to Persta and settled down to a quiet life with several of the fairest princesses of the realm.
At the age of 31 Alexander died of fever. Even If he had lived lenger there was nothing left for him to do but sit at a desk and run the country.
When we realize how pitiful It would have been to see the conqueror of the whole world and the most expert swordsman in history getting down behind a desk for twelve hours a day and wrapping his legs around a chair while trying to sign his name to a commission appointing an assistant weather observer at Babel-Mandeb, we can understand the kindness of Providence in removing Alexander before the politicians got at him.
Either Alexander was the greatest general the world has ever known or they had press agents in those days who make ours look dumb and bashful.
(Copyrighted by George Mathew Adams.)

headlines.title: Nothing Lost by Delay
headlines.fullText: Quasi-official advices from the storm center of government in Austin Indicate that the ancient populate favortie, initiative and referendum, is in trouble again. If the coptention of some of the legal talent that the amendment was not properly submitted should prove correct, then the faithful at the forks and elsewhere may not get to vote for it, even after waiting until November of next year, Of course Governor Colquitt could place the matter before the special session for correction of the records to comply with the requirements of the constitution, but the governor Is well Informed on the history of Pandora's famous box, and he would hardly be inclined to open the doors for promiscuous trouble in July and August when the patriotic fortitude of all hands will be sufficiently tried without a gummer flood of new bills. There appears, in taet, to be at least a reasonable probability that the delightful and excellent initiative and referendum may have to hang around in the brush somewhere until the regular session in January of 1915 fetches it out and starts it off again. et perfectly lovely, honest, unselfish popular government. This would be indeed a longevious, and to some a distressing, Incubation, procrastination and divagation, so to speak. Wildly enthusiastic referendumists may stand on the tiptoe of expectation until they become politically club-footed in the endurance test. The case in truly bad and sad, but there are some very mitigating etreumetances. Possibly the time lost In waiting for the Initiative and referendum amendment to be put in proper and legal shape may be wisdom gained for the conclusion that good old-fashioned representative democracy munnen comment stronger enough and of the right kind. None of the states that have the initiative, referendum and recall has 80 far demonstrated the vast superiority and value claimed for such devices; and it is possible the true secret of refining common terrestrial clay into celestat perfection of virtue has not yet been discovered. But if. In fact, in verity, this new form of direct legislation holds the secret, nothing need be tost through a little delay in reaching taw. Hence there is room for comfort and good cheer, both for those who long to greet resurgent populism with a mighty kiss and those who would like to swat it with a stout "Old Hickory" elub.

headlines.title: Why Aren't Dogs Muzzled?
headlines.fullText:
Has any one meen a muzzled dog?
Reports of hydrophobia continue to come in and the city has an ordinance which requires that a dog permitted on the street must either be muzzled or be held in leash.
Unmuzzled dogs are to be seen every day in every section of the city running at large.
Meantime a vigorous campaign is being carried on to get the dogs tagged, The city gets a dollar for the brass tag that is put on a dog. A dog catcher is sent out to sell these tags. Why doesn't the city turn the dog catcher's attention to picking up the unmuszied dogs, tag or no tag?
An untagged deg bites no worse than a tagged one. Let's not repeat the mistake that resulted in the Fast Ninth street riot. Several instances of mad dogs have been reported in the city. If more occur they will be due to the failure of the police department enforce the ordinance already on its books,
Muzzling the dogs is Important, and Uncle Bob has the courage to do it If someone just will stir him into action.
Maybe a few phone calls from the people In the neighborhood where mad dogs have been at large would be an effective means of getting the law enforced.

headlines.title: The Optimist
headlines.subtitle: BY REV. J. P. HICKS.
headlines.fullText:
WHY MEN LOVE DARKNESS.
"Men loved darkness rather than light, because their deeds were evil" John 3:13.
Light is a great crime extinguisher. It has been proven In densely populated districts In great eltles where crime has been prevalent that an are light would go farther toward extinguishing crime in the dark places than all the policemen in the city, The best way to drive crime out of a dark place is to turn on the light. One of the best ways of extending the interests of the church would be for every church in the city to endow a light in a certain dark place. Let each church erget hunt out per a dark place where liebt crime abounds and erect on that spot a great are light which will reflect throughout the dark place the light which eliminates crime. On this light could be placed the name of the church. The therefore would become a source of advertisement for the church which thus deals with darkness and it would also be doing missionary work while the members slept in their comfortable beds.
Men of means should be induced to erect lights in dark places that crime might be eliminated. It is true the city should do such work and does to a certain extent. But where the light is most needed It is often conspicuously absent. The city cannot have too many lights. It is the intention of the city to put in as many lights as will cease the clamoring of the people for more light. Hence the lights usually are lifted out in the refined and cultured centers of the city rather than in the dark and criminally inelined sections. The city would not raise any objection to a church er a Sunday school, or a young people's society or well-disposed Christian man or woman raising a great are light and maintaining it in some of the neglected portions of the city. On this 'light might be placed scriptural verses or mottoes which would cause the erring mind to think and turn away from sin's destructive ways. Who will be the first to place a light in a dark place? Let your light shine.
